verses.commentary: Do not believe every spirit. Discernment is commanded. Test the spirits to see if they are from God. Spiritual claims must be examined. Many false prophets have gone out into the world. Deception is prevalent. Gullibility is not faith. The church must test teaching against Scripture and apostolic truth. Not all supernatural activity is from God.

The test: does the spirit confess Jesus Christ has come in the flesh? Incarnation is the litmus test. The Gnostics denied Christ's true humanity. Acknowledging Jesus as fully God and fully man is essential orthodoxy. Confession here is not mere words but Spirit-inspired conviction. True teaching affirms the incarnation. The Spirit of God testifies to the incarnate Christ.

Every spirit that does not acknowledge Jesus is not from God. Denial of Christ's incarnation is antichrist. The spirit of antichrist is already in the world. False teaching is present, not just future. Christological heresy disqualifies a message as from God. The incarnation is non-negotiable. Deviation here is demonic, not divine.

You are from God and have overcome them. Victory over false teachers. The One in you (Holy Spirit) is greater than the one in the world (Satan). Spiritual warfare is real but the outcome is certain. Believers possess superior power through the indwelling Spirit. The devil is defeated; the Spirit is victorious. We overcome not by our strength but by the Spirit's power.

They (false teachers) are from the world. Their origin determines their message. They speak from the world's viewpoint, and the world listens to them. Worldly teaching attracts worldly people. Popularity with the world is not validation; it is often indictment. Truth is not determined by acceptance. False teaching resonates with fallen nature.

We (apostles) are from God. Knowing God produces receptivity to apostolic teaching. Whoever does not know God does not listen to us. Spiritual birth creates spiritual receptivity. This is how we recognize the Spirit of truth and the spirit of falsehood. Response to apostolic teaching reveals spiritual origin. Those born of God receive the truth; those of the world reject it. Doctrine divides.

Let us love one another. Love's source is God. Everyone who loves is born of God and knows God. Love is the fruit of regeneration. True love flows from divine origin. Knowing God and loving others are inseparable. Love is not human sentiment but divine nature reproduced in believers. Loving proves spiritual birth.

Whoever does not love does not know God. Lovelessness reveals absence of relationship with God. Because God is love. Not God has love, but God is love. Love is His essence. This is one of the Bible's most profound statements. Love is not an attribute among others; it defines who God is. To know Him is to love. To refuse love is to reject Him.

This is how God showed His love: He sent His one and only Son into the world. The incarnation and atonement are love's ultimate demonstration. Purpose: that we might live through Him. Love gives life. Without Christ, we are dead in sin. God's love is initiating, sacrificial, life-giving. The cross is love made visible.

This is love: not that we loved God, but that He loved us. Love originates with God, not humans. We are responders, not initiators. He sent His Son as an atoning sacrifice (hilasmos, ἱλασμός) for our sins. Propitiation: satisfying God's wrath. Love and justice meet at the cross. God's love does not ignore sin; it deals with it. The cross is the supreme act of love.

Since God so loved us, we also ought to love one another. Divine love obligates human love. Receiving God's love compels extending it. Ought (opheilō, ὀφείλω) implies moral duty, debt. Love is not optional. God's love to us becomes the pattern and motivation for our love to others. Vertical love produces horizontal love. We love because He first loved us (v. 19).

No one has ever seen God. He is invisible (John 1:18). But if we love one another, God lives in us and His love is made complete (teleioō, τελειόω) in us. Love for each other is the manifestation of God's presence. Invisible God becomes visible through believers' love. Love is the proof of indwelling. When Christians love, God's love reaches its full expression.

We know we live in Him and He in us by the Spirit He has given. The Holy Spirit is the assurance of mutual indwelling. The Spirit's presence confirms relationship. This echoes 3:24. The Spirit is God's deposit, guaranteeing our inheritance (2 Corinthians 1:22, Ephesians 1:14). Assurance is not subjective but Spirit-grounded.

We (apostles) have seen and testify that the Father sent the Son to be the Savior of the world. Eyewitness testimony to the incarnation and mission of Christ. Jesus is the universal Savior, not limited to Israel. The gospel is for the world. Salvation is in Christ alone but offered to all. The apostolic message is rooted in historical reality: they saw, heard, touched (1:1-3).

Acknowledging Jesus is the Son of God results in mutual indwelling: God lives in them and they in God. Confession of Christ's deity secures relationship with God. Jesus is not merely a teacher or prophet but the Son of God. Acknowledging Him as such is essential for salvation. Confession leads to communion. Right belief produces right relationship.

We know and rely on the love God has for us. Knowledge and trust. God is love: His essence restated. Whoever lives in love lives in God, and God in them. Love is the sphere of communion. Abiding in love is abiding in God. Living in love means practicing love toward others. This is the environment of fellowship with God. Love is not abstract theology but lived reality.

Love is made complete so we will have confidence on the day of judgment. Perfected love removes fear of condemnation. In this world we are like Jesus. Believers reflect Christ's character now. Present likeness to Christ ensures future boldness. Judgment day is not feared by those who abide in love. God's love in us prepares us for His judgment. Assurance flows from Christlikeness.

No fear in love. Perfect love drives out fear. Fear has to do with punishment. The one who fears is not made perfect in love. Fear and love are incompatible. Perfect (teleios, τέλειος) means complete, mature. Mature love casts out fear of judgment. This is not sinless perfection but confident assurance in God's love. Fear of punishment reveals incomplete understanding of God's love. Those secure in His love do not dread condemnation.

the effect. We are responders, not originators. His love enables and motivates our love. Without His prior love, we cannot love. This is the gospel in a sentence. Love flows from God to us, then through us to others. All love originates in God.

Claiming to love God while hating a brother or sister is a lie. Vertical love cannot coexist with horizontal hatred. For whoever does not love their brother or sister, whom they have seen, cannot love God, whom they have not seen. Logic: if we do not love the visible, we do not love the invisible. Love for people is the test of love for God. Hatred disqualifies religious profession. You cannot separate love for God from love for people.

This is His command: whoever loves God must also love their brother and sister. The two great commands united (Matthew 22:37-40). Loving God requires loving others. One cannot exist without the other. This is not suggestion but command. Love is the defining ethic of Christianity. Faith, hope, and love, but the greatest is love (1 Corinthians 13:13). Love fulfills the law.
